entities.description: OCR-derived electronic selection of only the three prose pieces in the 1969 Greenwood reprint. entities.description: The historically independent and separately covered paper component containing the eleven works edited by Lumby. Craft of Deyng is at ff. 1r-4r, Dicta Salomonis at ff. 5r-12r, and The Vertewis of the Mess at ff. 53r-54r.

holding.notes: Part of the present Kk.1.5 composite host; separately covered and historically independent before joining the neighboring Lancelot component.

reviewNotes: The cataloging boundary is exactly the three DIV1 prose works, not the broad source title, the eponymous Ratis Raving, the complete eleven-work manuscript part, or the complete print volume.
Current host and historical component are separate entities: Kk.1.5 is the composite holding object; part 6 was historically independent and remains separately covered.
The 1969 Greenwood carrier is the immediate print source of the U-M OCR; the 1870 Lumby edition is its reproduced predecessor, not silently the same physical edition.

King's working transcript, U-M's foliation microfilm, and Girvan's complete working rotograph are explicit intermediary source layers. The U-M microfilm was consulted only for milestone placement in Dicta Salomonis; Girvan transcribed through the separately modeled rotograph.

The Internet Archive PDF is complete as a 528-page compound provider object and contains Lumby's complete volume plus additional bound material; it is not described as a standalone 139-page edition scan.

The Ff.5.45 bundle is a complete supporting analogue cache with target_work_presence absent and no work_portion. Its ff. 24v-46r / canvases 60-103 range appears only in the analogue relation and notes.
Wynkyn de Worde/Lydgate material is comparison evidence only. Neither the early print nor Ff.5.45 is placed in the artifact's ancestry.
Public access, physical completeness, cached-file fixity, copyright status, licenses, and provider terms are separate assertions.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a pinned copy of a 1997 University of Michigan HTI electronic selection containing three independent Older Scots prose works: Craft of Deyng, Dicta Salomonis, and The Vertewis of the Mess. U-M OCRed and marked up selected pages from Greenwood Press's 1969 reprint of J. Rawson Lumby's 1870 EETS OS 43 edition; the call number and encoded title leaves establish the 1969 carrier even though the header bibliographic source statement gives the original 1870 imprint. Lumby worked from W. W. King's complete manuscript copy of Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5 part 6. That part is a separately covered historical component inside the current composite Kk.1.5 host and carries the selected works at ff. 1r-4r, 5r-12r, and 53r-54r. U-M separately consulted an unidentified manuscript microfilm to place folio milestones in Dicta Salomonis. Complete provider objects are cached for the compound Internet Archive scan containing Lumby, Girvan's complete 1939 edition, CUL's complete 172-canvas Ff.5.45 analogue manuscript, and the official CUL descriptions of Kk.1.5 and Ff.5.45. No complete public Kk.1.5 facsimile was found; official onsite and whole-object imaging-order routes are recorded. Ff.5.45 and Wynkyn de Worde's Vertue of the Masse are supporting analogues only, not ancestors of the selected texts.

catalogSummary.author: Three anonymous Older Scots translators or compilers
catalogSummary.date: Individual works composed in the fifteenth century; copied circa 1480-1490; first printed in 1870; exact electronic selection published in 1997
catalogSummary.editor: J. Rawson Lumby; three-piece electronic selection by the University of Michigan Humanities Text Initiative
catalogSummary.region: Lowland Scotland / Older Scots; exact composition places unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Three Prose Pieces from Ratis Raving and Other Moral and Religious Pieces

catalogingSubject.label: Three Prose Pieces from Ratis Raving and Other Moral and Religious Pieces
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the exact three-work electronic selection represented by RatisRav: Craft of Deyng, Dicta Salomonis (headed Wisdom of Solomon by Lumby), and The Vertewis of the Mess. It is not one medieval work, the eponymous Ratis Raving, all eleven works in Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5 part 6, or Lumby's complete 1870/Greenwood 1969 volume.

contentParts.label: Craft of Deyng

locators.value: pp. 1-8; PDF pp. 19-26; IA viewer n18-n25

notes: The XML represents the work with one P element; that encoding choice is not a claim that the work has one intellectual paragraph.
contentParts.summary: Anonymous Older Scots abridging translation of Ars Moriendi material.

contentParts.label: Dicta Salomonis (Wisdom of Solomon)

locators.value: pp. 11-25; PDF pp. 29-43; IA viewer n28-n42

notes: The explicit per manum V. de F. identifies the witness scribe's hand, not authorship of the work.
contentParts.summary: Anonymous Older Scots prose paraphrase of Ecclesiastes, probably through an unidentified French intermediary.

contentParts.label: The Vertewis of the Mess

locators.value: pp. 113-115; PDF pp. 131-133; IA viewer n130-n132

contentParts.summary: Anonymous prose collection of patristic and scriptural sayings about the virtues of hearing Mass.

dateStatements.display: After 1395, probably in the first half of the fifteenth century
Fifteenth century, before the circa 1480-1490 copy
Fifteenth century, before the circa 1480-1490 copy

notes: This dates the witness hand, not all three compositions.

scope.description: The three copies in CUL MS Kk.1.5 part 6

extent.description: The repository XML BODY contains three top-level DIV1 units numbered 1, 6, and 11, with 24 P elements distributed 1, 1, and 22 by component, and no L elements. Eight additional P elements occur in HEADER metadata, making 32 P elements file-wide. Paragraph elements are representation metrics, not intellectual item counts; the source manuscripts write the first two works as solid prose. The selection is complete relative to U-M's declared prose-only boundary.

formStatements.description: All three selected works are prose; the XML BODY contains 24 P elements (1, 1, and 22 by component) and no L elements. Eight additional P elements occur in HEADER metadata, for 32 P elements file-wide.

notes: The preferred title is intentionally supplied and boundary-explicit because the electronic item does not contain the eponymous Ratis Raving.
The three works have independent authorship, dates, genres, and source relations; manuscript juxtaposition and electronic selection do not create common medieval authorship.
Solomon, Gerson, Rolle, cited saints, and V. de F. are not cataloged as authors of the three works.

The current CUL host codex, its historically independent part 6, King's working transcript, Lumby's edition, Greenwood's reprint, U-M's encoding, and the repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.
Rights, provider terms, access, cache integrity, and dated no-public-facsimile findings are recorded in the companion lineage manifest.

next_steps: Collate the Scots work against complete texts of the Latin Ars Moriendi family and the two English analogues; do not infer direct descent from opening correspondence alone.
openQuestions.question: What exact Latin exemplar and textual relationship underlie Craft of Deyng and its fuller Middle English analogues in CUL MS Ff.5.45 and Bodleian MS Douce 322?

next_steps: Compare the paraphrase against medieval French Ecclesiastes traditions; retain V. de F. as an unidentified scribe, not an author.
openQuestions.question: Can the unidentified French intermediary proposed for Dicta Salomonis be identified, and can the identity of the scribe V. de F. be established?

next_steps: Collate its sayings against Simmons's parallels, Reliquiae Antiquae, and the Lydgate/de Worde tradition while preserving the current analogue-only relation.
openQuestions.question: Can the immediate source collection for The Vertewis of the Mess be identified?

next_steps: Use CUL's official imaging order route for the whole present codex if complete physical coverage is required; recheck CUDL periodically.
openQuestions.question: Can a complete lawful digital facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Kk.1.5 be obtained?

notes: A dialect and copying-context assertion, not an exact place of composition.

subjects.label: Purgatory, masses, and prayer for the dead

summaries.value: A bounded electronic selection of three independently composed fifteenth-century Older Scots prose works: an abridged art-of-dying treatise, a paraphrase of Ecclesiastes, and a compilation of attributed sayings on the spiritual and temporal benefits of hearing Mass.
The XML retains Lumby's item numbers 1, 6, and 11. The gaps are deliberate: U-M selected only the three prose pieces and excluded the eight verse pieces, including the eponymous Ratis Raving.
An anonymous Older Scots abridgment of the Latin Ars Moriendi tradition, treating the commendation of death, preparation for dying, faith, repentance, restitution, forgiveness, and final prayer.
An anonymous prose paraphrase of Ecclesiastes on vanity, wealth, labor, wisdom, mortality, judgment, and service to God, probably mediated by an unidentified French translation.
An anonymous compilation of sayings attributed to Christ, Paul, and patristic authorities about hearing Mass, including benefits for prayer, health, childbirth, purgatory, protection, and salvation.

Authorship, date, sources, and genre are scoped to the three component works. Print publication, manuscript copying, digital selection, and encoding are distinct events and are not inherited as common composition facts.

notes: The supplied title makes the three-work boundary explicit and avoids implying that the eponymous Ratis Raving is included.
